raw_result.description: Open-source CLI and Python package for finding usernames across social networks.
raw_result.auth_notes: The official README/docs evidence gathered describes local installation and CLI usage via pipx, Docker, distribution packages, and Python package metadata; no first-party developer/auth documentation for API authentication methods was found in the gathered evidence.
raw_result.gating_reason: The official GitHub repository and project website document open-source installation and usage with no signup, payment, admin approval, or partner gate indicated in the gathered evidence.

api_surface.breadth: The gathered first-party evidence indicates Sherlock is documented as a local CLI/Python package for username searches, with no documented REST or GraphQL API surface in the reviewed repository/docs evidence.
api_surface.mcp_notes: The gathered first-party evidence did not show an official Sherlock MCP server. A GitHub discussion mention was identified as a third-party integration rather than a first-party MCP offering.

raw_result.blocker: No documented public API surface or documented API authentication was found in the gathered first-party evidence; the project appears to be a CLI/library rather than an API-backed app toolkit.
raw_result.rate_limit_notes: No official API rate-limit documentation was found in the gathered evidence. As a CLI that queries many third-party sites, practical limits likely depend on target sites rather than a Sherlock-hosted API.
raw_result.notable_signals: Repository evidence indicates Python packaging and a CLI entry point; docs focus on usage/install rather than developer API access. The sherlock-project GitHub organization also contains separate repositories, but the gathered evidence did not establish a first-party public API for this app.
raw_result.human_review_reason: The structured field auth_methods is uncertain because the gathered first-party evidence did not provide a dedicated auth/developer page; absence of documented auth was inferred from README/docs focused on CLI usage rather than directly stated on an auth page.
